results.title: When sellCreditMarket() is called to sell credit for a specific cash amount, the protocol might receive a lower swapping fee than expected
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports the same fee-undercollection bug in the SellCreditMarket exactAmountIn=false path via AccountingLibrary.getCreditAmountIn. Items #12 and #17 state that fees are computed on a fee-adjusted (net) base instead of the gross present value, causing systematic under-collection when selling credit for a specific cash amount. This is the same function/code path (getCreditAmountIn used by SellCreditMarket with exactAmountIn=false) and the same root cause as the described issue. The proposed fixes (computing fees with a (PERCENT - swapFeePercent) denominator or computing on the raw PV) would resolve both the report’s and the description’s issue, so they are the same vulnerability.

results.title: Risk of overpayment due to race condition between repay and liquidateWithReplacement transactions
results.reasoning: The audit report does not mention the race condition between executeRepay and executeLiquidateWithReplacement that can cause a borrower to overpay by repaying after a replacement changes the borrower on the same debtPositionId. None of the findings reference executeRepay, borrower identity checks in repay, or the need to couple borrower with debtPositionId. The LiquidateWithReplacement-related findings concern fee recipient routing, missing swap fee, and tenor/minTenor constraints, which are different issues and fixes. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: The collateral remainder cap is incorrectly calculated during liquidation
results.reasoning: Not detected. The described bug is the incorrect collateral remainder cap in executeLiquidate: the cap is computed with crLiquidation instead of (crLiquidation - PERCENT), causing unfairly large protocol share for higher-CR borrowers. None of the audit items mention the collateral remainder cap or this formula error. The closest items (#4/#19) concern a unit mismatch in the liquidation reward cap, and #6 concerns a one-way ratchet on crLiquidation updates—different mechanisms, code paths, and fixes. Fixing the issues reported in the audit would not fix the remainder-cap miscalculation.

results.title: Users won’t liquidate positions because the logic used to calculate the liquidator’s profit is incorrect
results.reasoning: Yes. The report’s Findings #4 and #19 describe the same bug in executeLiquidate: the liquidation reward cap is computed with debtPosition.futureValue (borrow-token units) and compared against collateral-denominated values, resulting in a near-zero reward. They reference the same code path, mechanism (unit/decimal mismatch), impact (no incentive to liquidate), and propose the same fix (compute the cap using debtInCollateralToken). Fixing the report’s issue would fix the described vulnerability.
